text: ATTALENS, a village of Switzerland, on the SW confines of the canton of Friburg, 11 m. E of Lausanne.
ATTALIA. See ADALIA.
ATTAM, a large town on the r. bank of the Old Calabar river, in about N lat. 6° 40'. The river is here 200 yards wide, and bends to the S.—Becroft.
ATTANAGH, a parish partly in Queen's co., but chiefly in co. Kilkenny, 2 m. SE of Castle-Durrow. Area 2,621 acres. Pop. 919.
ATTANCOURT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Vassy, on the r. bank of the Blaise. Pop. 365. In the vicinity are chalybeate springs.
ATTARAM. See ATARAM.
ATTARUS (JEBEL), a mountain of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, and prov. of Kerek, about 9 m. W of the Dead sea, in N lat. 31° 42'.

ATTAVISI, an extensive district of Hindostan, in the southern division of the prov. of Gujerat, between the 20th and 21st parallels of N lat. It is intersected by numerous small streams, which take their rise in the W side of the Sydaree mountains. The principal towns are Bansa, Dhurrampur, and Daman. In 1802, - the period at which this district was mortgaged by Giucowar to the British,it yielded an annual revenue of 61 lacks of rupees. L ATT
ATTELE, a town of Prussian Westphalia, in the circle of Minden, 9 m. S of Paderborn. Pop. 1,000.
ATTENBOROUGH, a parish in Nottinghamshire. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 1,036.
ATTENDORN, a town of Prussian Westphalia, in the circle of Arnsberg, on the l. bank of the Bigge, 6 m. WNW of Bilstein. Pop. 1,498.
ATTERCLIFFE-WITH-DARNALL, a chapelry in the parish of Sheffield, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area. 1,270 acres. Pop. 4,156.
ATTERLEY, a township in the parish of Much Wenlock, Salop. Pop. 52.

ATTER-SEE, or KAMMER-SEE, a lake of Upper Austria, the largest in the archduchy, situated in the circle of Hausruck, 5 m. SSW of Vocklabruck. It is 12 m. in length from N to S; and averages 2 m. in breadth. On the SW it is connected by a narrow channel with the Mondsee; and on the N it discharges itself by the Agger into the Traun, a tributary of the Danube. It abounds with fish.
ATTERSOOMBA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, on the l. bank of the Wautruck, 30 m. E of Ahmedabad.

ATTERT, a town of Belgium, in the prov. and arrond. of Luxemburg, on the r. bank of a river of same name, an affluent of the Alzette, about 5 m. NNE of Arlon, and on the road between that town and Bastogne. Pop. 2,216.
ATTERTON, a hamlet in the parish of Witherley, Leicestershire. Pop. 84.

ATTICA, a district of the Grecian continent, which constituted the principal territory of the Athenian republic in ancient times; under Turkish domination it was comprised in the pashalik of Negropont; and it now forms, in nearly its ancient extent, a nomarchy of modern Greece. It is of a triangular form, extending between the strait of Euripus or Negropont on the NE, and the Saronic gulf, or gulf of Ægina, on the SW, from the ranges of Elatea or Cithæron, and Nosea or Parnes, which separates it from Bootia, on the N, to Cape Colonnes or Sunium, the vertex of the triangle, on the S. The eastern and western sides of the triangle are, with the exception of a small portion to the NW of Athens,-forming the small state of Megara, under the ancient divisions of Greece, which the range of Ægaleos divides from the plain of Athens,—watered by the Ægean sea. Its greatest length, between Cape Sunium to Cape Livadostro, is 50 m.; and its medium breadth about 27 m.; comprising a superficies of nearly 750 sq. m., an area scarcely equal to the smallest departments of France, or one of our average-sized English counties. The geological formation is primitive limestone, reposing on mica slate. The inclination of the slate is from 50° to 60°. Some serpentine also occurs. [Holland.] In many parts the calcareous rock projects above the surface. The surface, which is thinly clad with soil, is, to a great extent, mountainous and sterile. The mountains subside like waves of the sea; but seldom subside into fertile valleys: much is moor and rocky waste, enlivened only by a few spots in the neighbourhood of villages, where the heather yields to small patches of corn, and a few trees wave their greener branches over the parched soil, reminding the traveller of the wilder districts of Scotland. [Burgess.] The principal mountains belonging to this territory are Mendeli or Pentelicus, 10 m. to the NE of Athens, [alt. 3,637 ft.,] famous for its marbles; Hymettus or Tesso-Vuni and Mavro-Vuni, [alt. 3,341 ft.,] long celebrated for its honey; and the mountains of Laurini or Laurium, formerly rich in mines of silver, and now supposed to contain rich iron-mines. Although the olive was assiduously cultivated in the magnificent plains of Marathon and Eleusis, the principal occupation and wealth of the ancient Athenian 2 D A. was always esteemed for its purity, and is still the best in Greece, except on the marshy plains of the Cephissus. Byron describes the climate as a perpetual spring. Winter sets in about the beginning of January; towards the middle of that month a little snow occasionally falls; about the middle of February gentle rains fall; early in March the vine and olive are in bud, and the almond in blossom; in May harvest commences. The mountain-pine is found on the slopes of Laurium; and the steeps of Parnes and Pentelicus are sprinkled over with the dwarf-oak, the lentisk, the arbutus, and the bay; but there are few timber-trees. [Wordsworth.] Sunium is no longer a "woody promontory;" and it would be difficult to conceive a more dreary scene,-a more perfect waste of rock, heath, and mountain, unrelieved by forest or tree,—than is presented by the coast-line from Athens to Sunium. [Mure.] A considerable part of Parnes is covered with forests of pine, oak, arbutus, and wild pears. The agricultural produce of A., owing to the lightness of the soil, is confined to barley and olives. Barley ripens about 25 days earlier here than in the Morea and in Crete. The olives are considered larger in size and richer in flavour than any produced elsewhere. Throughout Greece, honey is more agreeable and aromatic than in other lands; but the honey of the Hymettus no longer possesses any superiority over that of other districts of Greece. The number of hives on the Hymettus and Pentelicus is about 5,000. [Fiedler.] The principal domestic animals are goats and sheep. The Athenians can no longer hunt bears in the forests of Lycabettus; but the rugged mountains about Marathon are frequented by wolves, foxes, and jackals. Game, especially hares and partridges, is abundant. The hooded crow is a constant inhabitant of A. The cuckoo is heard early in spring; the stork arrives in March, and leaves about the middle of August; and the merops appears at the end of summer. The goldfinch and the linnet enliven the groves of A. A small species of bat flutters about the ruins of Athens; and 'Minerva's bird,' -the Strix passerina,-still abounds in the city and its environs. The phoca and the porpoise are occasionally seen off the coasts. On the E coast of A. was the port of Prasia, now called Rafti; on the SW the port of Anagyronti, now Vari; and in the vicinity of Athens, the harbours of Phalerum, of Munychia, and the Peiraus. The last-mentioned port, built by Themistocles in 477 B. C., and destroyed by Sylla in 87, formed the marine arsenal, and the great commercial entrepot of the republic. See Athens. This territory contains no rivers of any importance: the streams which water it are torrents rather than rivers. The Cephissus to the W, and Ilissus to the E of Athens, flow through the plain into the Phaleric bay. The Erasinus descends from the eastern slope of Hymettus into the sea N of Rafti; and the plain of Marathon is watered by a stream descending from Mount Phelleres. Athens has been from time immemorial the only city of Attica. The principal towns, the ruins of which still exist, are: Eleusis, now Levsina, the city anciently next in rank to Athens, and famous for the celebration of the greater mysteries of Ceres; Decelea, a fortress at the entrance of a defile of Mount Parnes, near the modern village of Tatoi; Marathon, 10 m. NE of Athens; Anaphlystos, the modern Anaphiso; Besa, now Anabesi; and Anagyronti, now Vari. The entire pop. of Attica, in the year 319 B.C., seems to have amounted to about 500,000, of whom 71,000 resided in the city of Athens. Under Turkish sway, in 1794, the country was divided into 4 districts, namely, Messoïa, Catta Lama, Eleusina with Mount Casha, and the territory of the city of Athens. These districts contained about 60 villages, and about ATT 418 consisted in their maritime commerce. The air of 12,000 inhabitants, of whom 1,000 were Turks. [Sibthorp.] The pop. in 1836 was only 23,000.—Clarke's Travels. - Holland's Travels. - Burgess's Letters. - Strong's Greece.—Fiedler's Travels.

ATTICA, a township of New York, U.S., in Wyoming co., 257 m. W of Albany, and 375 from Washington. It is watered by the Tonawanda and its tributaries; and presents a generally undulating surface. Pop. 2,710. The village is situated on the l. bank of the Tonawanda. Pop. 800.-Also a township of Indiana, U. S., on the E side of the Wabash river, in Fountain co., 73 m. WNW of Indianopolis.
ATTICAMEONOS, a prov. of Lower Canada, to the NW of the district of Three Rivers, intersected from W to E by a series of small lakes, which discharge themselves by Ribbon river into the Maurice.

ATTICHY, or ATTICHI, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Oise, arrond. of Compiègne, on the N bank of the river Aisne, 12 m. W of Soissons. The town contains 200 houses, a castle, and a mineral spring. Its commerce consists principally in grain. Pop. 1,006.—The cant. comprises 20 communes. Pop. in 1831, 12,210.
ATTIEN, a village in the island of Cyprus, about 15 m. from Larnica, famous for its honey. The bees are kept in earthern cylinders, 3 ft. in length.
ATTIGNE VILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Neufchateau. Pop. 610.

ATTIGNY, or ATTIGNI, a canton, and ancient and once celebrated town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, and arrond. of Vouzieres, on the l. bank of the Aisne, 22 m. SW of Mezieres. The cant. comprises 12 com. The town consists of about 170 houses, and has a pop. of 1,162. Chilperic II. died here in 720; and here, in 822, the famous council was held at which penance was publicly performed by Louis I., in expiation of the murder of his nephew.

ATTINGHAUSEN, a village of Switzerland, in the canton of Uri, on the l. bank of the Reuss, about 1½ m. SW of Altdorf. It is celebrated as having been the residence of Walter Furst, father-in-law of the patriot William Tell.
ATTL, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Isar, on a river of the same name, near its junction with the Isar, about 3 m. SW of Wasserburg.

ATTLEBOROUGH, or ATTLEBURGH, a parish and town in Norfolkshire, 151 m. SW of Norwich. Area 5,800 acres. Pop. 1,959.—Also a hamlet in the parish of Nuneaton, in Warwickshire. Pop. 1,095.—Also a post-town of Massachusetts, U. S., in Bristol co., 30 m. S of Boston. It is watered by some of the affluents of the Pawtucket river. Pop. in 1840, 3,585. Iron-stone is found in the vicinity. -Also a post village in Pennsylvania, U. S., in Buck's co., on a tributary of the Delaware, 7 m. NNW of Bristol.
ATTLEBRIDGE, a parish in Norfolkshire, 5 m. SSE of Reepham. Area 810 acres. Pop. 94.
ATTLEBURY, a town of New York, U. S., in Dutchess co., 71 m. S of Albany.

ATTOCK, an important town and fortress of Hindostan, in the Punjab, on the slope of a low hillock on the E side of the Indus, in N lat 33° 56', and Elong. 72° 30', 45 m. ESE of Peshawer, and 210 m. NW of Lahore. Alt. above sea-level 1,700 ft. The modern fortress of A. is of a rectangular form, having the shortest faces, 1,200 ft. in length, parallel to the river; and measuring about double that extent from W to E. It is strongly built of polished stone; but is commanded by an adjacent hill from which it is only separated by a narrow ravine; and being on a slope, almost the whole of the interior, and the reverse of the walls on three faces, is visible from the opposite bank. A. was formerly the seat of the Lahore government; but after 1818, when it fell into the hands of Rajah Runjeet Singh, it rapidly declined in importance. It now contains about 2,000 inhabitants. The original fortress was built by Acbar about the year 1581. The passage of the Indus by Alexander in 326 B. C. is generally supposed to have been effected in the immediate vicinity of A .then called Taxila; and the same route is believed to have been adopted by Tamerlane in his invasion of Hindostan in 1398. A fort erected on the opposite side of the river by Nadir Shah in 1738 still exists. The appellation Attock, or Atak,—signifying 'limit,' or 'prohibition,'-is derived, according to some, from the restrictions which are here imposed on the navigation of the Indus; according to others, from the circumstance of its being on the original frontier of Hindostan, beyond which a Hindu might not pass without special permission. [Rennel.] This river,—here 260 yards in breadth in June, [Elphinstone], with a depth of 35 fath. and a current exceeding 6 m. an hour in the middle of March [Burnes]— is crossed by a bridge of 37 boats maintained by the rajah of Lahore for the transit of his troops. Such a bridge can only be thrown across the Indus from November to April, when the waters are low, and the velocity of the stream comparatively small. [Burnes.] It has been completed in three days, but six is the usual period. The banks consist of dark stone, smoothly polished by the impetuosity of the current; and immediately in front of the town are the famous rocks of Jemilia and Kemilia. About 200 yards above A., the river, narrowed to 120 yards, rushes over a rapid with great force, at the rate of 10 m. in the hour; but after the Cabul river has joined it, it becomes a tranquil stream, and flows calmly on to the confluence of the Hurrú river, where it again tumbles and roars among huge boulders and long ridgy ledges of rock.—Elphinstone's Cabul.—Burnes' Bokhara.— Wood's Narrative.
ATTOCK, a name given to a stripe of fertile country in Persia, watered by numerous rivulets, and once well-peopled, which stretches along the northern base of the Elburz range in Khorasan.
ATTOWA PIPLIA, a town of Hindostan, in the province of Malwah, situated on a nullah 24 m. SE of Dewas, and 9 m. WSW of Unchode. It contained in 1820 about 500 houses.

ATTRI, a large river of Hindostan, which takes its rise on the SW confines of Tibet, and, making its way through the Himalaya ranges, runs to the S under the name of the Teesta, separating Bhotan and Sikkim, its channel being in this quarter 800 yards wide. It then flows to the SE, receiving the accession of many streams; again assumes a S direction, and takes the appellation of Attri; and after a course of 400 m. enters the Pudda, or great eastern branch of the Ganges, at the town of Jaffiergunge, 42 m. WNW of Dacca. This river, in common with all the streams belonging to this region of Hindostan, is remarkable for the number of offsets by which its waters inosculate with adjacent rivers. Above and below Chilmary it communicates by several channels with the Brahmaputra.

ATTRUCK, a river of Persia, which rises from numerous streamlets descending from the north side of the Elburz mountains, and runs in a W direction through the Attock, until it is joined by the Sumban, when it curves to the SW, and falls into the Caspian at Kurusufi, about 40 m. NNW of Astrabad.

ATTU, an island in the Aleutian archipelago, in N lat. 53°, and E long. 173°. It is about 50 m. in length, and 20 m. in average breadth; and is covered to a great extent with lofty snow-clad mountains. It contains a few small rivers and lakes, but presents a general aspect of extreme sterility. Considerable numbers of the blue fox still exist in this island. AUB ATTUI, ATUI, ATOOI, or TAUAI, one of the Sandwich islands, in N lat. 22°, and W long. 159° 40', 75 m. NW of Oahu. It is a mountain-mass, sloping on all sides to the ocean, about 40 m. in length, and about half that extent in average breadth; and is remarkable for its fertility. The pop. is about 10,000. The principal settlements are in the neighbourhood of Waimea river. The inhabitants are a hardy and industrious race.
ATTUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, on the N side of the Vellaur river, 60 m. N of Trichinopoli.—Also another town of the Carnatic, in the district and 10 m. SW of Dindigul.

ATTYMAS, a parish in co. Mayo, 31 m. N of Foxford. Area 11,154 acres. Pop. 3,435. One of the Ox mountains in this p. attains an alt. of 1,005 ft. ATUMMAYO, ALTUMMAYO, or IMASSA, a river of Assuay, a tributary of the Amazon, which it joins in about S lat. 5°, after a course of 200 m. from SE to NW.
ATUNAUILLICAS, a town of Upper Peru, 40 m. W of Potosi, in the mountains of Los Charcas.
ATUPINSKOI (CAPE), a headland in Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, projecting into Behring's gulf, in N lat. 60° 50', E long. 174° 30'.

ATURES, a village on the Orinoco, in about N lat. 5° 35', in the vicinity of what are called the rundales of A., a series of narrow channels, amidst rocks and rocky islands, through which the Orinoco descends with great rapidity, and in a succession of small cascades, for about half-a-mile.
ATVIDABERG, a village of Sweden, 17 m. SE of Linköping, celebrated for the rich copper mines in its vicinity.
ATWATER, a town in Portage co., Ohio, U. S., 21 m. E of Akron. Pop. 755.
ATWICK, a parish on the coast of the E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 300.
ATWOOD'S KAY, or SAMANA, one of the Bahama islands, in N lat. 23° 5', W long. 73° 37'. It is 9 m. long from E to W; and 12 m. broad. Its highest point does not exceed 100 ft. in alt.
ATZGERSDORF, a town of Lower Austria, in the circle of Vienna. Pop. 1,900.

ATZMUS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Saint Gall, 2 m. N of Sargans, on the l. bank of the Rhine. AU, a village of Lower Austria, at the foot of the Leithagebirge, N of Vienna. Pop. 645. - Also a village of Tyrol, on the Aach, SE of Bregenz.—Also a village in the cant. of Aargau, on the l. bank of the Reuss.—Also a town in Upper Bavaria, in the circle of Isar, 10 m. NW of Mosburg, on the Albens. Pop. 645.—Also a village in the same circle, 14 m. N of Landshut.
AU-AM-RHEIN, a village of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, on the l. bank of the river. Pop. 1,000.
AUB, a village of Bavaria, in the Rezat circle, 16 m. SSE of Wartzburg, on the Gollach, an affluent of the Tauber. Pop. 1,020.

AUBAGNE, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. of Marseilles. The cant. comprises 4 com. The town is 9 m. E of Marseilles, on the road to Toulon. It trades chiefly in tiles and pottery, and wine. It was the birth-place of the Abbé Barthélemi. Pop. 6,350.
AUBAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Sommieres. Pop. 1,500.
AUBAN (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Grasse. The cant. comprises 13 com. The com., 14 m. NW of Grasse, has a pop. of 650.
AUBANGE, a commune and village of Belgium, in Luxemburg, 5 m. S of Arlon. Pop. 1,024.

AUBE, one of the central departments of France, composed of the southern part of the ancient prov. of Champagne, and a small portion of Burgundy. It is bounded on the N by the dep. of Marne; on the E by that of Upper Marne; on the SE by Côted'Or; on the S and SW by Yonne; and on the NW by the dep. of the Seine and Marne. Its greatest length from E to W is 72 m.; and its breadth 47 m. It comprises a superficial area of 609,000 hectares; and stretches from 47° 55' to 48° 42' N lat.; and from 3° 24' to 4° 48' E long. It is situated, throughout nearly its entire extent, within the basin of the Seine, and presents, with little irregularity of surface, a general inclination from SE to NW. The valley of the river, from which the dep. takes its name, extends in the greater part of its course in a direction nearly parallel to that of the Seine. The SW districts are watered by the Vannes, and other inferior tributaries of the Yonne. The principal affluents of the Seine are; on the r. the Ourse Barse, and, on the NW confines of the dep., the Aube; on the l. the Laignes and the Lozein. The Seine is navigable as far as Troyes, the cap. of the dep. The etang or lake of Villemaheu, the largest in the dep., covers a surface of 150 hectares. In the character of its soil, this dep. presents two remarkably distinct regions. Towards the N and NW, its general aspect is that of sterility,—the scantiness, except in the valleys, of the vegetable mould, scarcely sufficing here to cover the chalky substratum beneath; while in the S and E, it presents rocky hills luxuriantly covered with vines, and interspersed with valleys of great fertility. The climate is humid and variable; but mild and salubrious. The prevailing winds are the NW and SW. Notwithstanding the extensive tracts of waste land which this dep. comprises, about two-thirds of the surface admit of the culture of grain; about one-sixth forms permanent meadow-land; and nearly one-twenty-seventh is devoted to the growth of the vine; two-thirtieths are covered with wood, consisting chiefly of oak, yolk-elm, beech, aspen, and birch. Considerable plantations of Scottish fir now exist in the chalky districts of the N. The principal agricultural productions of the dep. are buck-wheat, rye, oats, rape-seed, and potatoes. The principal wine districts are those of the Riceys, of Gravilhers, and Bar-sur-Aube. About onehalf of the produce in wine is exported. The number of horses is estimated at 40,000, while that of cattle does not exceed 46,000, and of sheep 180,000. Fowls and pigs are extensively reared, but the supplies of the latter are not adequate to the local consumption. The culture of bees forms an important branch of industry. The open country abounds with various species of small game; and the forests with wild-boars, stags, and roebucks. The dep. possesses quarries of marble, lithographic-stones, paving-stone, and chalk,—which, in its manufactured state, known as Spanish whiting, forms an important article of commerce, -and clay. The manufactured produce consists principally in cotton, linen, and woollen fabrics. There are also potteries, glass and tile-works, manufactories of delft-ware, paper and hydraulic sawmills, rope-works, distilleries, sugar-houses, &c. The commerce of export consists chiefly in grain, horses, cattle, hemp, yarn, wool, staff-wood, casks, hoops, baskets, cordage, &c. The dep. of A. is comprehended in the 18th military division, and forms, with the dep. of Yonne, the 8th forest-preserve. Troyes, the cap., is the see of a bishop, suffragan of the archbishop of Sens. At Clairvaux is an ancient and celebrated abbey, and a central house-of-correction for the thirteen surrounding departments.—A. is divided into the five arrond. of Troyes, Arcis-sur-Aube, Bar-sur- Aube, Bar-sur-Seine, and Nogent-sur-Seine, com- Pop. 3,515. AUB prising 26 cantons and 447 communes. The pop. in 1846 was 261,881; in 1836, 253,870.—The ter. ritory of A. was anciently inhabited by the Tricasses. Monuments both of the Roman and Gallic supremacy still exist in various localities. In 1814 it was the scene of several conflicts between the French and the allied powers.

AUBE, a river of France, which takes its rise near Pralay, about 18 m. W of Langres, in Upper Marne, and runs NW, tracing for a short distance the confines of the dep. of the Côte-d'Or; thence, bending to the N, it enters the dep. to which it gives its name. Near the town of Bar it resumes a NW direction; and, after a brief passage through the SW extremity of the dep. of Marne, unites, on the confines of that of Aube, with the Seine. The entire length of the A. is 120 m., only 24 of which are navigable. Its principal affluents, all of which are on the right, are the Aujun, Voire, Puis, and Auges. The chief articles of transit on these rivers are charcoal, timber, and fire-wood, from the dep. of the Upper Marne and Vosges, grain, &c.
AUBEL, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Liege, arrond. and 10 m. N of Verviers. Pop. 3,300. Important grain-markets are held here.

AUBENAS, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, arrond. of Privas. The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 17,855 in 1831. The com. is on the r. bank of the Ardeche, 16 m. SW of Privas. Pop. 4,759. Fruits, truffles, silk, and wines, form the chief produce of the environs.
AUBENCHEUL-AU-BAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Cambrai. Pop. 471.
AUBENTON, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Vervins. The cant. comprises 13 com. The com. is 14 m. E of Vervins. Pop. 1,623.
AUBEPIERRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Arc-en-Barrois. Pop. 1,469.
AUBERCHICOURT, a commune of France, dep. of Nord, cant. of Douai. Pop. 1,240.
AUBERG, a town of Austria, on the N bank of the Danube, opposite to Lintz.
AUBERIVE, a canton and commune of France, dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. and 14 m. W by S of Langres. Pop. of com. 540. The cant. comprises 29 com.
AUBERIVES-EN-ROYANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Pont-en-Royans. Pop. 694.
AUBERS, a commune of France, dep. of Nord, cant. of La Bassée. Pop. 1,457.
AUBERT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Carnieres. Pop. 2,249.—Also a district in the co. of Beauce, in Lower Canada, on the Chaudiere.
AUBERTIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenées, cant. of Lasseube. Pop. 1,065.
AUBERVILLIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Saint-Denis, 4 m. N of Paris. Pop. 2,230.
AUBETERRE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Charente. The cant. comprises 13 com. The com. is on the Dronne river, 7 m. SE of Barbezieux. Pop. 763.
AUBETSIES, a river of Michigan, flowing into Lake Michigan in N lat. 44° 30'.
AUBETTE, a stream of France, flowing into the Seine at Rouen, after a course of 10 m.
AUBIAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Aigueperse. Pop. 1,338.
AUBIERE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. and 2 m. S of Clermont-Ferrand.
AUBIERS (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Chatillon-sur-Sevre. Pop. 1,876.
AUBIET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Gimont. Pop. 1,474.
AUBIGNAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, arrond. and 3 m. NW of Carpentras. Pop. 1,717.
AUBIGNE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille - et - Vilaine, cant. of Saint Aubin - d'Aubigne. Pop. 129.—Also a com. in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Mayet. Pop. 1,954.

AUBIGNY, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais. The cant. comprises 30 com. The com., 14 m. E of Saint-Pol, has a pop. of 678.—Also a commune in the dep. of Haute- Marne, cant. of Prauthoy. Pop. 228.—Also a town of Lower Canada, in the co. of Dorchester, opposite Quebec, of which it may be considered a suburb.
AUBIGNY-AU-BAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Arleux. Pop. 1,214.

AUBIGNY (VILLE), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Sancerre. The cant. comprises 5 com. The com. has a pop. of 2,169. It is situated on the Nère, a tributary of the Saldre, 18 m. N of Bourges. The dukes of Richmond inherit the lordship of Aubigny from their ancestress, the duchess of Portsmouth.

AUBIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Thitorville. in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Villefranche. The cant. comprises 9 com. The town is 18 m. NE of Villefranche. Rich coal-mines are wrought in the vicinity; and alum and sulphur also are obtained here. Pop. 3,392.

AUBIN (SAINT), a town in the island of Jersey, in the parish of St. Brelade, on the western side of a bay of the same name, 31 m. from St. Helier on the opposite side of the bay. It consists of about 100 houses, "partly skirting the shore, and partly lying on the rocky and well-wooded heights that, from the backs of the houses, drop perpendicularly into the sea." [Inglis.] The bay is open to winds between SSE and WNW. The tides rise and fall upwards of 40 ft. in the bay. The beach consists of a fine whitish sand, remarkably firm and smooth.

AUBIN (Saint), the name of several communes and villages in France, viz.—In Gironde, cant. of Saint-Ciers-la-Lande. Pop. 849. In Jura, cant. of Chemin. Pop. 1,559. In Landes, cant. of Mugron. Pop. 959. In Loiret, cant. of La Ferte-Saint-Aubin. Pop. 1,744. In Meuse, cant. of Commercy. Pop. 621. In Nièvre, cant. of La Charité. Pop. 1,082.— Also a village of Switzerland, in Neufchatel, 5 m. SW of Boudry. Pop. 1,000.
AUBIN-D'AUBIGNE' (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et- Vilaine, arrond. of Rennes. The cant. comprises 14 com. The town is 10 m. NNE of Rennes. Pop. 1,300.
AUBIN-DE-BEAUBIGNE' (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sèvres, cant. of Chatillon. Pop. 1,313.
AUBIN-JOUXTE-BOULLENG (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Elbœuf. Pop. 1,444.
AUBIN - EN - BRAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Le Coudray- Saint-Germer. Pop. 473.
AUBIN-DES-CHATEAUX (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Chateaubriant. Pop. 1,810.
AUBIN-LE-CLOUX (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux Sevres, cant. of Scerndigny. Pop. 1,240.

AUBIN-DU-CORMIER (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et- AUB Vilaine, and arrond. of Fougères. The cant. comprises 10 communes, the town is 10 m. SW of Fougères. Pop. 1,729.
AUBIN-DES-COUDRAIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of La Ferté Bernard. Pop. 1,133.
AUBIN-D'ECROSVILLE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Le Neubourgh. Pop. 1,005.
AUBIN-EPINAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine Inferieure, cant. of Rouen. Pop. 489.
AUBIN-FOSSE-LOUVAIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. and cant. of Mayenne. Pop. 1,000.
AUBIN-SUR-GAILLON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Gaillon. Pop. 1,079.
AUBIN-DE-LOCQUENAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Fresnay-le- Vicomte. Pop. 1,260.
AUBIN-DE-LUIGNE' (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Chalonnes. Pop. 1,525.
AUBIN - DU - PAVOIL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Segrè. Pop. 1,050.
AUBIN-DE-SCELLON (SAINT), a commune of Pop. 1,419.
AUBIN-DE-TERRE-GATTE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Saint James. Pop. 1,874.
AUBIN-DE-THENNAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Broglie. Pop. 1,097.

AUBONNE, a town of Switzerland, cant. of Vaud, 11 m. SSW of Lausanne, on a stream of the same name which falls into the lake of Geneva. Pop. 1,534. Byron says of this place that "it commands by far the fairest view of the lake of Geneva," from which it is about 3 m. distant. The environs produce excellent wine.
AUBOUE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Briey. Pop. 724.
AUBOURN, a parish of Lincolnshire. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 374.
AUBOUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Garlin. Pop. 268. Excellent white wine is made here.
AUBRAC, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, 12 m. E by N of Espalion, in the com. of Saint-Chely-d'Aubrac. Pop. 175. The ruins of a celebrated abbey founded in 1031 exist here.
AUBRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Valenciennes. Pop. 711. Coal is wrought here.
AUBRY-LE-PANTHOU, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Vimoutier. Pop. 425.
AUBURN, or Lishov, a village in co. Westmeath, 6 m. E of Athlone, generally supposed to be the scene of Goldsmith's poem 'The Deserted Village.'

AUBURN, the cap. of Cayuga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 173 m. W of Albany, on the outlet of Owasco lake. Pop. 5,626. There is a Presbyterian academy here founded in 1821. One of the two state-prisons is built here. It contains 555 cells, each 7 ft. long, 7 ft. high, and 31 ft. wide; arranged in 5 rows, or stories, opening into galleries. The convicts work together during the day, but are compelled to observe strict silence; and when not at work, or at their meals, are locked-up in their cells. The annual proceeds of their labour usually exceeds the expenses of the establishment. A bible, the only book allowed the prisoners, is placed in each cell; and the chaplain is the only person with whom the prisoners are allowed to hold communication.— Also a township in Worcester co., Massachusetts, 5 m. S by W of Worcester. Pop. 669.—Also a township in Geauga co., Ohio, 156 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 1,198.—Also a village in Susquehanna co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,113.

AUBUSSON, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Creuse.— The arrond. has an area of 205,371 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 105,106; in 1846, 106,795. It comprises the 10 cantons of Aubusson, Auzances, Bellegarde, Chénerailles, La Courtine, Crocq, Evaux, Felletin, Gentioux, and Saint - Sulpice - les - Champs. — The cant. comprises 12 com.—The town is situated on the Creuse river, 20 m. SE of Gueret. Pop. of the com. in 1836, 5,631. Its chief manufacture is that of carpets. It is also the commercial entrepot between Limoges and Clermont.
AUBY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Douai. Pop. 968.
AUCANVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Verdun-sur-Garonne. Pop. 1,100.

AUCH, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gers.—The arrond. has an area of 128,035 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 61,214; in 1846, 62,959. It comprises the 5 cantons of Auch, Gimont, Jegun, Saramos, and Vic-Fezenzac. —The cant. comprises 33 com.—The town and com. are on the l. bank of the Gers, a tributary of the Garonne, in N lat. 43° 38′ 39″, 410 m. SSW of Paris. Pop. in 1836, 10,461; in 1846, 9,474. A. is an ancient town, the Ausco of the 4th cent., and the Augusta Auscorum of the Romans. It is built in the form of an amphitheatre on a rising ground which is crowned by the cathedral of Sainte-Marie, one of the finest Gothic edifices in France, celebrated for the beauty of its vaulted roof, and the richness of its painted windows. It is divided into a lower and upper town, which are connected by a long flight of steps. It has some manufactures of woollen and cotton stuffs; its chief articles of trade are wines, quills, Bon Chretien pears, and brandy. A. is the seat of an archbishop, whose dio. comprehends the dep. of Gers, and whose suffragans are the bishops of Aire, Tarbes, and Bayonne.
AUCHENAIRN, a village in the parish of Cadder, in Lanarkshire, 3 m. N by E of Glasgow. Pop. 561.
AUCHENCAIRN, a village in the parish of Rerwick, Kirkcudbrightshire, 7 m. E by S of Kirkcudbright. Pop. 373.
AUCHINBLAE, a village in the parish of Fordoun, Kincardineshire. Pop. 643.

AUCHINDOIR AND KEARN, a large and mountainous parish in the W of Aberdeenshire, watered by the Bogie. The Buck of Cabrach, over which its W boundary runs, has an alt. of 2,286 ft. Pop. in 1841, 1,188. Houses 257.

AUCHINLECK, a parish of Ayrshire, 16 m. in length, by only 2 in average breadth. Pop. in 1831, <math>1,662</math>; in 1841, <math>1,659</math>. The v. of A. is <math>1\frac{1}{2}</math> m. from New Cumnock, and 15 from Ayr. Pop. 600. Coal, lime, and iron-stone abound in this p.; and a railway for the conveyance of minerals between A. and Muirkirk is now executing. It will be a single line of 11 m. in length.
AUCHINLOCH, a village in the parish of Cadder, in Lanarkshire, 2 m. S of Kirkintilloch. Pop. 90.
AUCHMEDDEN, a fishing village in the parish of Aberdour, in Aberdeenshire, 31 m. WNW of Aberdeen.
AUCHMITHY, a fishing village in the parish of St. Vigeans, in Forfar, 33 m. NE of Arbroath. AUC

AUCHTERARDER, a parish in Perthshire, on the Ruthven, a branch of the Earn. Pop. in 1831, 3,315; in 1841, 3,434. Craigussie, one of the Ochil range, alt. 2,359 ft., is in this p. The town of A. is on the line of the Scottish Central railway, by which it is 13½ m. distant from Perth, and 49 m. from Glasgow. In 1841 it contained 288 houses, and a pop. of 2,068. A large proportion of the pop. is employed in weaving for Glasgow houses.
AUCHTERDERRAN, a parish in Fifeshire, on the water of Orr. Pop. in 1831, 1,590; in 1841, 1,913, of whom 770 were in the v. of Lochgelly.

AUCHTERGAVEN, a parish in Perthshire, intersected by the Tay, and skirted on the N by an overlying range of the Grampians. It has an area of upwards of 20,000 acres. Pop. in 1841, 3,366, of whom 1,426 were in that part of the village of Stanley which is in this parish; 760 in the village of Bankfort; 133 in Carniehill; and 117 in Waterloo.
AUCHTERHOUSE, a parish in the SW of Forfarshire, about 4½ m. long, by 3½ m. broad. Pop. in 1841, 769.
AUCHTERLESS, a parish in Aberdeenshire, intersected by the Ithan. Pop. 1,685.
AUCHTERMUCHTY, a parish and borough in Fifeshire. Pop. of p. in 1841, 3,356, of whom 2,394 were in the town of A., which is on the road from Cupar to Kinross.
AUCHTERTOUL, a parish in the S of Fifeshire. Pop. in 1841, 530, of whom 239 were in the village of A.
AUCHY-EN-BRAY, a village of France, in the dep. of Oise, 21 m. WNW of Beauvair.
AUCHY-LES-MOINES, a village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, 15 m. W by N of Saint Pol, near the Ternoise. Pop. 1,000.

AUCKLAND (St. Andrew), a parish in Darlington ward, in the co. of Durham, intersected by the Bishop Auckland and Weardale railway. Area 45,420 acres. Pop. in 1831, 11,137; in 1841, 19,100. The great increase of pop. is ascribed to the opening of new collieries and iron-works, and the formation of a railroad in this district.

AUCKLAND (BISHOP'S), a township and markettown in the above parish. Area of township 2,570 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,961; in 1841, 3,776. The town is 10 m. SW of Durham, near the confluence of the Gaunless with the Wear. It is a neat wellbuilt town, and has some manufactories of muslin and cotton goods. It derives its name from its vicinity to the palace of the bishop of Durham. The Stockton and Darlington railroad passes near the town; and the Bishop Auckland and Weardale railway here runs off from this line to Frosterley. The length of the latter line is 16 m. 24 chains.

AUCKLAND (St. Helen), a chapelry and v. in the parish of Auckland St. Andrew, 3 m. SSW of Bishop Auckland. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 720. Raby castle, the noble seat of the Earl of Darlington, is within half-a-mile of the v.
AUCKLAND (West), a township and v. in the parish of Auckland St. Andrew, 4 m. SW of Bishop Auckland. Area 3,720 acres. Pop. 2,310.

AUCKLAND, the cap. of New Zealand, situated on the W coast of the northern island, on the southern bank, and about 2 m. from the entrance of the river Waitemata, in S lat. 36° 51' 27", E long. 174° 45' 20". [Stanley.] Pop. in 1842, 2,895; in 1847, 5,217. The town (A) is situated in the middle of the harbour, the river extending about 5 m. to the W, and terminating in a large bay, b, into which several small streams flow, one of which runs about 7 m. S towards Manakoa harbour, m, and takes boats at high water to its source, within 12 m. of that inlet. To the S, within 2 m. of A., is Mount Eden, e, alt. 500 ft., from the summit of which there is a beautiful i and extensive view over the town and along the N shores of the Waitemata. The land opposite the town, on the N shore, forms a peninsula, on which the North-head rises 280 ft. above sea-level, and supplies a signal-station to communicate to the town the arrival of ships in the offing. Beyond the E boundary of the A. district is a large bay, called Hobson's bay, h, and by the natives Orakei, on the shores of which a considerable body of natives are settled, who cultivate potatoes, maize, and melons, with which, and with fire-wood, they supply the town. About 5 m. to the E of A. is the estuary of the Tamaki, t, which has a spit of sand across its entrance, but deep water for some miles above. The island of Rangetoto, r, is a mass of volcanic scoria, rising to an alt. of 1,000 ft., and interspersed with thick low woods. m There is easy communication with the coast both to the N and S, and a considerable coasting-trade is already formed here. The first English merchant vessel that anchored in the Waitemata arrived on the 13th of Sept., 1840. The number of vessels which entered inwards in 1843 was 33, of a total tonnage of 6,065 tons; in 1844, the tonnage entering inwards was 5,010 tons; in 1845, 9,685 tons. The exports in 1843 amounted to £14,943; in 1845, to £45,411. The imports in 1845 amounted to £58,863. The surrounding country is slightly undulating. In the immediate neighbourhood there is little wood; but the richest and most available land in the island is concentrated within a radius of 50 m. around A.; and the town is well-supplied with fresh water, which is readily obtained by digging a few feet below the surface. The shore on which A. stands consists of cliffs of a soft pepper-coloured sandstone, or sandstone conglomerate, with occasional seams of lignite. Several volcanic cones rise in the immediate neighbourhood of the town, at the base of which hard scoriæ for building and for roads can be obtained. The sandstone too, though soft, hardens by exposure to the air, and supplies a good building-material. Frosts are rare; the mean temp. of the summer months is 67°; of the winter months 52°; the mean annual temp. 59°. In the northern hemisphere Montpellier, in N lat. 43° 36', E long. 3° 52', corresponds in mean annual temp. to A.; but in Montpellier the difference between the hottest and the coldest month is 68°, whereas in A. it is only 19°8. The heat of the warmest month in A. (January 69°) corresponds to that of the warmest month at Vienna, which is situated in N lat. 48° 12', E long. 16° 22'; its coldest month (July 49°.5) is somewhat below the temp. of Lisbon, in N lat. 38° 43', W long. 13° 49'. Gales of wind are frequent, and usually last for three days at a time. The most settled weather is with southerly winds. The variation of the compass is 14°; dip of the needle 61° 7′. The usual rise and fall | the favourite food of the wild hogs, viz., the Aralia of the tides is 10 ft.—Parl. Papers.—Dieffenbach's New Zealand, 1843.—Terry's New Zealand, 1842.

AUCKLAND ISLANDS, a group of islands in the Southern ocean, in S lat. 51°, E long. 166°, about 160 m. from the southernmost of the New Zealand group. They contain about 100,000 acres of good land; and consist of an island about the size of the isle of Wight, and several smaller islets, from which the principal island is separated by narrow channels. These islands remained unknown to Cook, though he penetrated many degrees beyond their lat.; the honour of their discovery, which took place in 1806, was reserved for Captain Bristow, of the whaling ship Ocean, belonging to the late Samuel Enderby, Esq. In the year after their discovery, Captain Bristow again visited the group, in the whaling ship Sarah, and then took possession of them in the name of the British Crown; leaving at the same time some domestic pigs, whose descendants are said to be very numerous. The Aucklands were visited in succession, in 1840, by the ships of the American, French, and English South-polar Exploring Expeditions, under Wilkes, D'Urville, and Ross. These authorities concur in describing them as of volcanic formation, constituted chiefly of basalt and greenstone. Sir James Ross rounded the group at its northern extreme. He describes the NW cape as "a very remarkable headland, with a rocky islet and a curious conical rock off it." He says "there is ample space for entering the harbour, and no concealed dangers, the belts of sea-weed, - Macrocystus and Laminaria,which line the shores and rocks, serving to point out the shallow and dangerous parts." The larger island contains two principal harbours, whose entrances are both from the eastward, and whose heads or terminations reach within 2 or 3 m. of the western coast, and are only 5 or 6 m. from each other. The upper part of the former, which is described as containing several secure anchorages, is called Laurie harbour; and Captain Ross was "so struck with the many advantages the place possesses for a penal settlement over every other he had heard named, to which to remove convicts from the surrounding colonies, that he addressed a letter on the subject to Sir John Franklin, on his return to Hobart Town, recommending its adoption." D'Urville, speaking of this harbour, says, "It is one of the finest that I know." A marked difference exists between the W and E coasts: the former presenting towards the sea a line of bluff, perpendicular cliffs, with some high hills in the background,—whereas, the latter, as described by D'Urville, exhibits "here and there a fine sandy beach, upon which the sea scarcely broke, and intersected by numerous streams and inlets." Deas-head, a promontory of the larger island, exhibits fine columns, 300 ft. high, which are highly magnetic. According to Sir James Ross, the loftiest hill, Mount Eden, attains an elevation of 1,300 ft., is rounded at the top, and clothed with grass to its summit; while another hill in the W rises nearly to 1,000 ft. The soil is described as being "generally good, and composed of a rich black mould, in many places of considerable depth—the result of decomposition of the volcanic debris and a redundant vegetation—so highly productive that it would render the island well worth the attention of colonists." The botany of the A. islands is characteristic of New Zealand, though containing many new forms typical of the Antarctic regions. Dr. Hooker remarks that "the whole land seemed covered with vegetation, and a low forest skirts all the shores, succeeded by a broad belt of brushwood, above which, to the summits of the hills, extend grassy slopes." Dr. H. mentions two remarkable plants, very common near the sea, and constituting polaris and the Pleurophyllum criniferum. The ornithology appears to be limited to seven or eight species of land-birds, all belonging to New Zealand, and to embrace a great variety of water-birds. There are no venomous reptiles; but the sand-flies are found troublesome, and their stings painful. A grant of exclusive possession of the A. islands has been made by the British government to Messrs. Enderby, "in consideration of the endeavours of their late father and themselves to promote geographical knowledge, and to extend the whale-fisheries;" and the proprietors have projected the formation of a jointstock establishment here, to prosecute the southern whale-fisheries. By the plan of operations contemplated, the necessity of employing expensive ships of large tonnage in the fishery—as is the case from Europe and America-would be entirely superseded, since the vessels so to be employed would not, as now, bring home their produce, but deposit it in store at the island-station, whence it would be reshipped to England or elsewhere. "Thus, there would always be ships on the whaling-grounds, or returning thence with produce to the station; always supplies of oils awaiting shipment to England; and always full cargoes on the way thither. There would, in fact, be a constant stream of produce flowing, so to speak, into the station from the various fisheries, and out of it to England; consequently, as the supply of oil in the home-market would thus be both regular and abundant, prices would be moderate and steady. By these means, a very considerable saving of expense would be effected, not only in the outset, but in the whole course of ulterior proceedings, and a system be established beyond the risk of failure." [Pamphlet of Mr. Enderby.] The advantages of the station seem to be beyond question, both as to position and harbours, and climate and soil. And the results a well-conducted enterprise of the kind is capable of yielding, may be understood from what has been achieved by the Americans. "Our fleet of whaling ships," it was observed by a member of the United States legislature in 1844, "is larger than ever pursued the business before. Commercial history furnishes no account of any parallel; our ships now outnumber those of all other nations combined; and the proceeds of its enterprise are in proportion, and diffused to every part of our country." Messrs. Enderby state that the Americans employ in the Southern whale-fishery between 600 and 700 ships, the greater part of which are 400 tons burden, and which are manned by from 18,000 to 20,000 seamen. From the year 1838 to 1845, the produce of the American whale-fishery has averaged 37,459 tuns, whereof 13,406 tuns have been exported, and 24,053 tuns retained for home consumption. In the last-mentioned year the produce amounted to no less than 43,064 tuns, representing, at the average American prices (though probably realizing much more), a value of £1,420,447; whereas, in the same year, the produce of the British fishery, including Greenland, was only 5,564 tuns, representing, at the average British prices, a value of £249,181, thus showing a balance of £1,171,266 in favour of American enterprise; a contrast which is anything but flattering to England, considering her maritime pretensions. The colony or settlement at the Auckland islands, to which the prosecution of the fishery, in the way indicated, would of necessity give rise, is to be exclusively a whaling one, the land being gradually brought under cultivation to supply as far as possible its wants, and thus made to add to the profits of the general enterprise. It is to be hoped that the proposal now put forward will meet with a favourable reception and a fair trial. "By a large majority of persons," says Commander Wilkes, "it is believed that the whale-fishery AUD is a mere lottery, in which success is more owing to good luck than to good management. Those, however, who entertain such an opinion are in error." He is of opinion that by an increased attention to the great currents of the ocean,-upon which he made a variety of experiments during his expedition,-the pursuit of whales may be rescued from the hap-hazard character it has hitherto borne. It is now well-known that molluscous animals are the food of the whale; and the shoals of gelatinous medusæ are swept along by those mighty oceanic streams, the limits and direction of which are proved to be ascertainable, and regulated, -as all Nature's operations are, -by uniform laws. Tracing out these streams, and commenting, as he dwells upon each, on their course, character, and causes, Mr. Wilkes indicates the best cruizing grounds and the most appropriate seasons for visiting them respectively. There is ample room in the vast spaces pointed out as the resort of whales for the largest fleets to operate without interfering with each other, and without any reasonable fear of exhausting the supply. A notion prevails in the United States with respect to the sperm-whales, similar to that which we occasionally hear from the masters of ships engaged in the Greenland and Davis straits fisheries, -namely, that the whales are diminishing in numbers. Commander Wilkes took pains to inquire into this alleged diminution, and thinks the opinion is not well-founded:-"They have indeed become wilder, or, as some of the whalers express it, 'more scary,' and in consequence not so easy to capture; but if we consider the numbers that continue yearly to be taken, there will, I think, be no reason to suppose that any great decrease has occurred. On an average, it requires 50 whales to fill a ship, and it would therefore take about 5,000 whales annually to supply the quantity of oil that is imported into the United States. This would appear but a small proportionate number, if these animals were as prolific as our herds on shore, when it is considered that they have a feeding-ground of 20,000,000 sq. m."

AUCUN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées, arrond. of Argeles. The cant. comprises 10 com. The com. lies on the Gave d'Azun, 9 m. W by S of Argeles, at an alt. of 3,075 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 823.
AUCUTTA, one of the Laccadive group, in N lat. 10° 52', E long. 72° 21'. It is 3 m. in length, by m. in breadth; and is connected by a reef at its S extremity with the island of Calputy.

AUDAH (SERJ-EL-), a branch of the Jebel Maunah, in the prov. of Constantina, in Algiers, near the r. bank of the Sebus. The town of Kalemah is built on one of its extreme spurs; and as this town appears to be on the site of the Calama of the Romans, which Orosius identifies with the Suthul of Sallust, the mountain may be the "præruptus mons" of the latter historian.

AUDE, a maritime department of France, consisting of a portion of the ancient province of Languedoc; bounded on the N by the dep. of Tarn; on the NE by that of Herault; on the E by the Mediterranean; on the S by the dep. of the Pyrénées Orientales; on the SW and W by that of Ariege; and on the NW by the dep. of Haut Garonne. It is situated between N lat. 42° 40' and 43° 30'. Its greatest length, in a direction nearly E and W, is about 81 m.; and greatest breadth from N to S, 60 m; comprising a superficial area of 606,397 hectares, or 1,498,396 acres. The dep. is traversed from S to N on its western boundary, by a mountain-range of medium height, uniting the Pyrenees with the Black mountains, the western portion of the Cevennes, which skirts the northern frontier. Another chain, called the Cerbieres, a ramification of the Pyrenees. stretches along the southern district in the direction of SW to NE. This dep. derives its name from its principal river, the Aude, which flows from S to N, until it reaches Carcassonne, when it takes an easterly direction, and falls into the sea, on the confines of the dep. of Herault. The chief tributaries of this river are, on the l., the Rebenti, Cougain, Son, Rougeane, Orbiel, Argent-Double, and the Ceysse; on the r., the Guette, Salse, and Orbieu. With the exception of the Aude, none of these rivers are navigable. A narrow strip on the W belongs to the basin of the Garonne, and is intersected by the Lers. The canal of Languedoc, which traverses the dep. from E to W, forms an important medium of communication between the Garonne and the Mediterranean. The coast, which is low and shallow, forms numerous lagunes, the principal of which are, those of Sijean, Palme, and Leucate. In the lower districts, the soil is generally calcareous, with an admixture of clay and silex. The climate is very variable. The prevailing winds are from the WNW, and from the sea. The former, designated the cers, blows during the three first months of the year; and returns periodically in July and August. It is bracing and salubrious, and highly favourable to vegetation. The latter, though mild near the coast, increases in impetuosity as it progresses inland.—The agriculture of this dep. is in a flourishing condition. More than four-ninths of the soil are employed in the cultivation of grain, and one-twelfth is appropriated to the vine. The meadows, which are extensively irrigated, cover an extent of about 16,731 hectares; the waste lands occupy about two-sevenths of the dep. The plains produce various descriptions of grain, but the more elevated districts admit only of the culture of buck-wheat and millet. The vine and the olive are extensively cultivated, especially the former. The principal forest-trees consist of the oak, beech, ash, pine, and fir. The shores produce kali and glasswort. The number of sheep belonging to the dep. is estimated at 578,000; of cattle, at 26,000; of horses, which are of an inferior description, at 10,000; mules, 20,000. Geese are extensively raised for home-consumption; and the honey of this district is of the finest quality. Game of every species is abundant. The woods are inhabited by various kinds of wild animals, such as the chamois, bear, wild bear, wolf, fox, and badger. The coast and all the rivers abound with fish. The mineral wealth of A. is considerable. There are mines of iron, copper, lead, magnesia, cobalt, and antimony. Besides the fine marbles of Cascastel, and of Cannes, there are quarries of lithographic stones, gypsum, limestone, slate, and freestone. At Tuchan and at Durban coal of excellent quality is obtained. The mountains contain many mineral springs both cold and thermal.— The principal manufactures are fine cloth, and other varieties of woollen fabrics, cheese, combs, jet ornaments, and casks. There are also paper-mills, distilleries, tanneries, iron and salt-works. The commerce consists of grain, wine, brandy, essences, honey, olive-oil, raisins, marine salt, silk, woollen fabrics, hosiery, jet, iron, copper, and alum, cattle, brakel. pigs, horses, and mules. The number of fairs which takes place in this dep. amounts to 166; they are held in 74 of the communes.—The dep. of Aude is divided into the four arrondissements of Carcassonne, Castelnaudry, Narbonne, and Limoux; comprising 31 cant. and 434 com. It forms the dio. of Carcassonne, the bishop of which is a suffragan of the archbishop of Toulouse and Narbonne; and it is under the jurisdiction of the assize court of Montpelier. The pop. in 1801 amounted to 225,228; in 1836 to 281,088; in 1846 to 284,285, or 46.88 to a square kilometre; the average density for the whole king- AUD dom being 64.868 to a sq. kil. Carcassonne is the cap. of this department.—A. originally formed a part of the ancient territory of the Tectosages. In 633 it became a Roman prov. At the commencement of the 5th cent. it fell under the dominion of the Goths, by whom it was retained for nearly 300 years. In 759 it was taken by Pepin le Bref, and was subsequently under the successive dominion of the dukes of Leptimaine, and the counts of Toulouse, until the reign of Louis VIII.—Barante, Essais sur le dep. de l'Aude. 8vo. 1803.—Annuaire de la dep. de l'Aude.—Dict. Geographique.

AUDE, a river in the above dep. which rises near the village of Angles, in the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, 6 m. NW of Mont-Louis, flows from S to N to Carcassonne; then runs from W to E, separating the dep. of Aude from that of Herault, and flows into the Mediterranean at the lagune of Vendres, after a course of about 140 m. in which it passes Quillan, Alet, Limoux, Carcassonne, Trebes, and Coursan.
AUDELANGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Rochefort. Pop. 246.

AUDENARDE, or OUDENAARDEN, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, the cap. of the arrond. of the same name. It is pleasantly situated in a valley on the r. bank of the Scheldt, 16 m. SSW of Ghent, in N lat. 50° 51', and E long. 3° 35'. Pop. 5,900. The principal architectural monuments of antiquity which still adorn this town are the church of Notre-Dame de Pamele, erected in the beginning of the 13th cent., and which is deemed one of the finest specimens of the transition style in Belgium; and the church of St. Walburge, belonging to the same era, but which underwent considerable alterations in the 14th cent. The town-hall, a Gothic structure of the 16th cent., is remarkable for the chaste elegance of its architecture; the porch and chimney-piece of the sheriff's chamber are chefsd'œuvres of Paul van der Schelden. The manufactures of A. consist chiefly in calicoes, in tapestry, nankin, ribbons, hats, soap, and pottery; there are also calico-printing works, tanneries, breweries, bleacheries, and salt-refineries. Cloth of different descriptions forms the principal article of commerce. A. is within the jurisdiction of Brussels, and concurs in the nomination of 2 deputies. — Various opinions exist with respect to the origin of this town. By Flemish historians it is ascribed to the Goths so early as the 5th cent. In 1053 a fortress-destroyed in 1783—was built by Count Boudouin de Lille, but it was not until the 12th cent. that A. acquired the status and privileges of a town. During the middle ages it underwent several memorable sieges; and on the 11th July 1708, the famous engagement between the allied troops, under Prince Eugene and the Duke of Marlborough, and the French, took place before its walls. In 1745 its fortifications were destroyed by the French, but they have since been partially restored. A. is celebrated as the birth-place of Adrian Brawer and of Margaret of Parma.—The arrond. of Audenarde is divided into 9 cantons, viz., Audenarde, subdivided into 2, Marie Hoorebeke, Renaix, Herzele, Sotteghene, Grammont, Ninove, and Neder-
AUDENÆS, a town of Norway situated near a cape of the same name, at the northern extremity of the island of Andoen, one of the Loffoden group, in N lat. 60° 28′, and E long. 15° 10′.

AUDENGE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and arrond. of Bordeaux. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,539. The village is situated at the upper extremity of the Bassin d'Arcachon, about 22 m. SW of Bordeaux, and possesses a small fishing-port. In the vicinity are saline marshes. Pop. 1,148.
AUDENHOVE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the province of East Flanders, 9 m. SE of Audenarde. Some linen is made here. Pop. 2,260.
AUDENSHAW, a township in the parish of Ashton-under-Lyne, in Lancashire, 42 m. E by S of Manchester. Pop. in 1841, 5,374, of whom 917 were in the village of A.

AUDEUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Besançon. The cant. comprises 44 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,441. It produces good wines, and has a saline spring. The village, 8 m. WNW of Besançon, has a pop. of 160.

AUDIERNE, a town of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Pontcroix, on a bay and at the mouth of a river of the same name, 20 m. W of Quimper. It has a good harbour and extensive fisheries, and enjoys an active commerce in corn and dried fish. Lace-making is carried on here to a considerable extent. Pop. 1,333.
AUDIGNON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of St. Sever. It produces good wines. Pop. 641.
